title: Mining

infobox.value: is the [extraction](/wiki/Resource_extraction "Resource extraction") of geological materials and minerals from the surface of the [Earth](/wiki/Earth "Earth"). Mining is required to obtain most materials that cannot be grown through [agricultural processes](/wiki/Agriculture "Agriculture"), or feasibly created [artificially](/wiki/Chemical_synthesis "Chemical synthesis") in a [laboratory](/wiki/Laboratory "Laboratory") or factory. [Ores](/wiki/Ore "Ore") recovered by mining include [metals](/wiki/Metal#Extraction "Metal"), [coal](/wiki/Coal "Coal"), [oil shale](/wiki/Oil_shale "Oil shale"), [gemstones](/wiki/Gemstone "Gemstone"), [limestone](/wiki/Limestone "Limestone"), [chalk](/wiki/Chalk_mining "Chalk mining"), [dimension stone](/wiki/Dimension_stone "Dimension stone"), [rock salt](/wiki/Rock_salt "Rock salt"), [potash](/wiki/Potash "Potash"), [gravel](/wiki/Gravel "Gravel"), and [clay](/wiki/Clay "Clay"). The ore must be a rock or [mineral](/wiki/Mineral "Mineral") that contains a valuable constituent, can be extracted or mined, and sold for profit.[[1]](#cite_note-1) Mining in a wider sense includes extraction of any [non-renewable resource](/wiki/Non-renewable_resource "Non-renewable resource") such as [petroleum](/wiki/Petroleum "Petroleum"), [natural gas](/wiki/Natural_gas "Natural gas"), or even [water](/wiki/Fossil_water "Fossil water").

[![Person stabbing a yellow mineral block](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/43/Sulphur_Mining_at_Kawah_Ijen_3.jpg/500px-Sulphur_Mining_at_Kawah_Ijen_3.jpg)](/wiki/File:Sulphur_Mining_at_Kawah_Ijen_3.jpg)

Mining of [sulfur](/wiki/Sulfur "Sulfur") from a [deposit](/wiki/Deposition_(geology) "Deposition (geology)") at the edge of [Ijen](/wiki/Ijen "Ijen")'s crater lake, Indonesia

**Mining** is the [extraction](/wiki/Resource_extraction "Resource extraction") of geological materials and minerals from the surface of the [Earth](/wiki/Earth "Earth"). Mining is required to obtain most materials that cannot be grown through [agricultural processes](/wiki/Agriculture "Agriculture"), or feasibly created [artificially](/wiki/Chemical_synthesis "Chemical synthesis") in a [laboratory](/wiki/Laboratory "Laboratory") or factory. [Ores](/wiki/Ore "Ore") recovered by mining include [metals](/wiki/Metal#Extraction "Metal"), [coal](/wiki/Coal "Coal"), [oil shale](/wiki/Oil_shale "Oil shale"), [gemstones](/wiki/Gemstone "Gemstone"), [limestone](/wiki/Limestone "Limestone"), [chalk](/wiki/Chalk_mining "Chalk mining"), [dimension stone](/wiki/Dimension_stone "Dimension stone"), [rock salt](/wiki/Rock_salt "Rock salt"), [potash](/wiki/Potash "Potash"), [gravel](/wiki/Gravel "Gravel"), and [clay](/wiki/Clay "Clay"). The ore must be a rock or [mineral](/wiki/Mineral "Mineral") that contains a valuable constituent, can be extracted or mined, and sold for profit.[[1]](#cite_note-1) Mining in a wider sense includes extraction of any [non-renewable resource](/wiki/Non-renewable_resource "Non-renewable resource") such as [petroleum](/wiki/Petroleum "Petroleum"), [natural gas](/wiki/Natural_gas "Natural gas"), or even [water](/wiki/Fossil_water "Fossil water").

Modern mining processes involve [prospecting](/wiki/Prospecting "Prospecting") for ore bodies, analysis of the profit potential of a proposed mine, extraction of the desired materials, and final [reclamation](/wiki/Mine_reclamation "Mine reclamation") or restoration of the land after the mine is closed.[[2]](#cite_note-2) Mining materials are often obtained from ore bodies, [lodes](/wiki/Lode "Lode"), [veins](/wiki/Vein_(geology) "Vein (geology)"), [seams](/wiki/Coal_mining "Coal mining"), [reefs](/wiki/Quartz_reef_mining "Quartz reef mining"), or [placer deposits](/wiki/Placer_deposit "Placer deposit"). The exploitation of these deposits for [raw materials](/wiki/Raw_material "Raw material") is dependent on investment, labor, energy, refining, and transportation costs.

Mining operations can have negative environmental impacts, both during mining and after the mine closes. Hence, most of the world's nations have enacted regulations to reduce the impact; however, the outsized role of mining in generating business for often rural, remote, or economically depressed communities means that governments frequently fail to enforce them fully. [Work safety](/wiki/Mine_safety "Mine safety") has long been a concern as well, and where enforced, modern practices have significantly improved safety in mines. Unregulated, poorly regulated or [illegal mining](/wiki/Illegal_mining "Illegal mining"), especially in [developing economies](/wiki/Developing_economies "Developing economies"), frequently contributes to local [human rights](/wiki/Human_rights "Human rights") violations and [environmental conflicts](/wiki/Environmental_conflict "Environmental conflict"). Mining can also perpetuate political instability through [resource conflicts](/wiki/Conflict_resource "Conflict resource").

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mining&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

### Prehistory

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mining&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Prehistory")]

Since the beginning of [civilization](/wiki/Civilization "Civilization"), people have used [stone](/wiki/Rock_(geology) "Rock (geology)"), [clay](/wiki/Clay "Clay"), and, later, [metals](/wiki/Metal "Metal") found near the Earth's surface. These were used to make early tools and weapons; for example, high-quality [flint](/wiki/Flint "Flint") found in northern [France](/wiki/France "France"), southern [England](/wiki/England "England"), and [Poland](/wiki/Poland "Poland") was used to create [flint tools](/wiki/Flint_(tool) "Flint (tool)").[[3]](#cite_note-3) [Flint](/wiki/Flint "Flint") mines have been found in [chalk](/wiki/Chalk "Chalk") areas where seams of the stone were followed underground by [shafts](/wiki/Shaft_sinking "Shaft sinking") and galleries. The mines at [Grimes Graves](/wiki/Grimes_Graves "Grimes Graves") and [Krzemionki](/wiki/Krzemionki "Krzemionki") are especially famous, and like most other flint mines, are [Neolithic](/wiki/Neolithic "Neolithic") in origin (c. 4000–3000 BC). Other hard rocks mined or collected for axes included the [greenstone](/wiki/Greenschist "Greenschist") of the [Langdale axe industry](/wiki/Langdale_axe_industry "Langdale axe industry") based in the [English Lake District](/wiki/English_Lake_District "English Lake District").[[4]](#cite_note-KloproggePonceLoomis2020-4)
The oldest-known mine in the archaeological record is the [Ngwenya Mine](/wiki/Ngwenya_Mine "Ngwenya Mine") in [Eswatini (Swaziland)](/wiki/Eswatini_(Swaziland) "Eswatini (Swaziland)"), which [radiocarbon dating](/wiki/Radiocarbon_dating "Radiocarbon dating") indicates is about 43,000 years old. At this site [Paleolithic](/wiki/Paleolithic "Paleolithic") humans mined [hematite](/wiki/Hematite "Hematite") to make the red [pigment](/wiki/Pigment "Pigment") [ochre](/wiki/Ochre "Ochre").[[5]](#cite_note-5)[[6]](#cite_note-6) Mines of a similar age in [Hungary](/wiki/Hungary "Hungary") are believed to be sites where [Neanderthals](/wiki/Neanderthal "Neanderthal") may have mined flint for weapons and tools.[[7]](#cite_note-7)

### Ancient Egypt

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mining&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Ancient Egypt")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/55/Malachite%2C_Zaire.jpg/250px-Malachite%2C_Zaire.jpg)](/wiki/File:Malachite,_Zaire.jpg)

Malachite

[Ancient Egyptians](/wiki/Ancient_Egypt "Ancient Egypt") mined [malachite](/wiki/Malachite "Malachite") at [Maadi](/wiki/Maadi "Maadi").[[8]](#cite_note-8) At first, [Egyptians](/wiki/Egyptians "Egyptians") used the bright green malachite stones for ornamentations and [pottery](/wiki/Pottery "Pottery"). Later, between 2613 and 2494 BC, large building projects required expeditions abroad to the [Wadi Maghareh](/wiki/Wadi_Maghareh "Wadi Maghareh") region to secure minerals and other resources not available in Egypt itself.[[9]](#cite_note-Shaw-9) Quarries for [turquoise](/wiki/Turquoise "Turquoise") and [copper](/wiki/Copper "Copper") were also found at [Wadi Hammamat](/wiki/Wadi_Hammamat "Wadi Hammamat"), [Tura](/wiki/Tura,_Egypt "Tura, Egypt"), [Aswan](/wiki/Aswan "Aswan") and various other [Nubian](/wiki/Nubia "Nubia") sites, on the [Sinai Peninsula](/wiki/Sinai_Peninsula "Sinai Peninsula"), and at [Timna](/wiki/Timna_Valley "Timna Valley").[[9]](#cite_note-Shaw-9) Quarries for [gypsum](/wiki/Gypsum "Gypsum") were found at the Umm el-Sawwan site; gypsum was used to make funerary items for private tombs. Other minerals mined in Egypt from the Old Kingdom (2649–2134 BC) until the Roman Period (30 BC-AD 395) including [granite](/wiki/Granite "Granite"), [sandstone](/wiki/Sandstone "Sandstone"), [limestone](/wiki/Limestone "Limestone"), [basalt](/wiki/Basalt "Basalt"), [travertine](/wiki/Travertine "Travertine"), [gneiss](/wiki/Gneiss "Gneiss"), [galena](/wiki/Galena "Galena"), and [amethyst](/wiki/Amethyst "Amethyst").[[10]](#cite_note-10)

[Mining in Egypt](/wiki/Mining_in_Egypt "Mining in Egypt") occurred in the earliest dynasties. The [gold mines of Nubia](/wiki/Nubia#Kerma "Nubia") were among the most extensive of any in Ancient Egypt. These mines are described by the [Greek](/wiki/Greeks "Greeks") author [Diodorus Siculus](/wiki/Diodorus_Siculus "Diodorus Siculus"), who mentions [fire-setting](/wiki/Fire-setting "Fire-setting") as one method used to break down the hard rock holding the [gold](/wiki/Gold "Gold"). One of the complexes is shown in one of the earliest known mining maps.[[11]](#cite_note-11) The miners crushed the ore and ground it to a fine powder before washing the powder for the gold dust known as the dry and wet attachment processes.[[12]](#cite_note-12)

### Ancient Greece and Rome

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mining&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Ancient Greece and Rome")]

Further information: [Mining in Roman Britain](/wiki/Mining_in_Roman_Britain "Mining in Roman Britain")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c4/Dolaucothimap4.jpg/500px-Dolaucothimap4.jpg)](/wiki/File:Dolaucothimap4.jpg)

Ancient Roman development of the [Dolaucothi Gold Mines](/wiki/Dolaucothi_Gold_Mines "Dolaucothi Gold Mines"), Wales

Mining in Europe has a very long history. Examples include the silver mines of [Laurium](/wiki/Laurium "Laurium"), which helped support the Greek [city state](/wiki/City-state "City-state") of [Athens](/wiki/Athens "Athens"). Although they had over 20,000 [slaves](/wiki/Slaves "Slaves") working them, their technology was essentially identical to their [Bronze Age](/wiki/Bronze_Age "Bronze Age") predecessors.[[13]](#cite_note-13) At other mines, such as on the island of [Thassos](/wiki/Thasos "Thasos"), marble was quarried by the [Parians](/wiki/Paros "Paros") after they arrived in the 7th century BC.[[14]](#cite_note-14) The marble was shipped away and was later found by [archaeologists](/wiki/Archaeologists "Archaeologists") to have been used in buildings including the tomb of Amphipolis. [Philip II of Macedon](/wiki/Philip_II_of_Macedon "Philip II of Macedon"), the father of [Alexander the Great](/wiki/Alexander_the_Great "Alexander the Great"), captured the gold mines of Mount Pangeo in 357 BC to fund his military campaigns.[[15]](#cite_note-15) He also captured gold mines in [Thrace](/wiki/Eastern_Macedonia_and_Thrace "Eastern Macedonia and Thrace") for minting coinage, eventually producing 26 [tons](/wiki/Ton "Ton") per year.

However, it was the [Romans](/wiki/Ancient_Rome "Ancient Rome") who developed large-scale mining methods, especially the use of large volumes of water brought to the minehead by numerous [aqueducts](/wiki/Aqueduct_(watercourse) "Aqueduct (watercourse)"). The water was used for a variety of purposes, including removing overburden and rock debris (a process called [hydraulic mining](/wiki/Hydraulic_mining "Hydraulic mining")), washing [comminuted](/wiki/Comminution "Comminution") (or crushed) ores, and driving simple machinery.

The Romans used hydraulic mining methods on a large scale to prospect for the [veins](/wiki/Vein_(geology) "Vein (geology)") of ore, especially using a now-obsolete form of mining known as [hushing](/wiki/Hushing "Hushing"). They built numerous aqueducts to supply water to the minehead, where the water was stored in large [reservoirs](/wiki/Reservoirs "Reservoirs") and tanks. When a full tank was opened, the flood of water [sluiced](/wiki/Sluice "Sluice") away the [overburden](/wiki/Overburden "Overburden") to expose the [bedrock](/wiki/Bedrock "Bedrock") underneath and any gold-bearing veins. The rock was then worked by [fire-setting](/wiki/Fire-setting "Fire-setting"), heating it and then quenching it with a stream of water. The resulting [thermal shock](/wiki/Thermal_shock "Thermal shock") cracked the rock, enabling it to be removed by further streams of water from the overhead tanks. The Roman miners used similar methods to work [cassiterite](/wiki/Cassiterite "Cassiterite") deposits in [Cornwall](/wiki/Cornwall "Cornwall") and [lead](/wiki/Lead "Lead") ore in the [Pennines](/wiki/Pennines "Pennines").

Sluicing methods were developed by the Romans in [Spain](/wiki/Spain "Spain") in 25 AD to exploit large [alluvial](/wiki/Alluvial "Alluvial") gold deposits, the largest site being at [Las Medulas](/wiki/Las_Medulas "Las Medulas"), where seven long aqueducts tapped local rivers and sluiced the deposits. The Romans also exploited the silver present in the [argentiferous galena](/wiki/Galena "Galena") in the [mines of Cartagena](/wiki/Mines_of_Cartagena "Mines of Cartagena") (*[Cartago Nova](/wiki/Cartago_Nova "Cartago Nova")*), [Linares](/wiki/Linares,_Ja%C3%A9n "Linares, Jaén") (*Castulo*), [Plasenzuela](/wiki/Plasenzuela "Plasenzuela") and [Azuaga](/wiki/Azuaga "Azuaga"), among many others.[[16]](#cite_note-16) [Spain](/wiki/Spain "Spain") was one of the most important mining regions, but all regions of the [Roman Empire](/wiki/Roman_Empire "Roman Empire") were exploited. In [Great Britain](/wiki/Great_Britain "Great Britain") the natives had mined minerals for [millennia](/wiki/Millennia "Millennia"),[[17]](#cite_note-17) but after the [Roman conquest](/wiki/Roman_conquest_of_Britain "Roman conquest of Britain"), the scale of the operations increased dramatically, as the Romans needed [Britannia](/wiki/Britannia "Britannia")'s resources, especially [gold](/wiki/Gold "Gold"), [silver](/wiki/Silver "Silver"), [tin](/wiki/Tin "Tin"), and [lead](/wiki/Lead "Lead").

Roman techniques were not limited to surface mining. They followed the ore veins underground once opencast mining was no longer feasible. At [Dolaucothi](/wiki/Dolaucothi "Dolaucothi") they [stoped](/wiki/Stoping "Stoping") out the veins and drove [adits](/wiki/Adit "Adit") through bare rock to drain the stopes. The same adits were also used to ventilate the workings, especially important when [fire-setting](/wiki/Fire-setting "Fire-setting") was used. At other parts of the site, they penetrated the [water table](/wiki/Water_table "Water table") and dewatered the mines using several kinds of machines, especially [reverse overshot water-wheels](/wiki/Reverse_overshot_water-wheel "Reverse overshot water-wheel"). These were used extensively in the [copper](/wiki/Copper "Copper") mines at [Rio Tinto](/wiki/Rio_Tinto_(river) "Rio Tinto (river)") in Spain, where one sequence comprised 16 such wheels arranged in pairs, and lifting water about 24 metres (79 ft). They worked as treadmills with miners standing on the top slats. Many examples of such devices have been found in old Roman mines, and some examples are now preserved in the [British Museum](/wiki/British_Museum "British Museum") and the [National Museum of Wales](/wiki/National_Museum_of_Wales "National Museum of Wales").[[18]](#cite_note-18)

### Medieval Europe

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mining&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Medieval Europe")]

Main article: [Mining and metallurgy in medieval Europe](/wiki/Mining_and_metallurgy_in_medieval_Europe "Mining and metallurgy in medieval Europe")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/63/Georgius_Agricola.jpg/250px-Georgius_Agricola.jpg)](/wiki/File:Georgius_Agricola.jpg)

Agricola, author of *[De Re Metallica](/wiki/De_Re_Metallica "De Re Metallica")*

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c4/Gallery_medieval_silver_mine_Germany_southern_Black_Forest_12th_century.jpg/250px-Gallery_medieval_silver_mine_Germany_southern_Black_Forest_12th_century.jpg)](/wiki/File:Gallery_medieval_silver_mine_Germany_southern_Black_Forest_12th_century.jpg)

Gallery, 12th to 13th century, Germany

Mining as an industry underwent dramatic changes in [medieval Europe](/wiki/Middle_Ages "Middle Ages"). The mining industry in the early [Middle Ages](/wiki/Middle_Ages "Middle Ages") was primarily focused on extracting [copper](/wiki/Copper "Copper") and [iron](/wiki/Iron "Iron"). Other [precious metals](/wiki/Precious_metal "Precious metal") were also used, mainly for gilding or coinage. Initially, many metals were obtained through [open-pit mining](/wiki/Open-pit_mining "Open-pit mining"), and [ore](/wiki/Ore "Ore") was primarily extracted from shallow depths, rather than through deep mine shafts. Around the [14th century](/wiki/14th_century "14th century"), the growing use of [weapons](/wiki/Weapon "Weapon"), [armour](/wiki/Armour "Armour"), [stirrups](/wiki/Stirrup "Stirrup"), and [horseshoes](/wiki/Horseshoe "Horseshoe") greatly increased the demand for iron. Medieval [knights](/wiki/Knight "Knight"), for example, were often laden with up to 100 pounds (45 kg) of plate or [chain link armour](/wiki/Chain_mail "Chain mail") in addition to [swords](/wiki/Sword "Sword"), [lances](/wiki/Lances "Lances") and other weapons.[[19]](#cite_note-19) The overwhelming dependency on iron for military purposes spurred iron production and extraction processes.

The silver crisis of 1465 occurred when all mines had reached depths at which the shafts could no longer be pumped dry with the available technology.[[20]](#cite_note-20) Although an increased use of [banknotes](/wiki/Banknote "Banknote"), credit and [copper](/wiki/Copper "Copper") [coins](/wiki/Coin "Coin") during this period did decrease the value of, and dependence on, [precious metals](/wiki/Precious_metal "Precious metal"), [gold](/wiki/Gold "Gold") and [silver](/wiki/Silver "Silver") remained vital to the story of medieval mining.

Due to differences in social structure, the increasing extraction of mineral deposits spread from [central Europe](/wiki/Central_Europe "Central Europe") to England in the mid-sixteenth century. On the continent, mineral deposits belonged to the crown, and this regalian right was stoutly maintained. But in England, royal mining rights were restricted to [gold](/wiki/Gold "Gold") and [silver](/wiki/Silver "Silver") (of which England had virtually no deposits) by a judicial decision of 1568 and a law in 1688. [England](/wiki/England "England") had [iron](/wiki/Iron "Iron"), [zinc](/wiki/Zinc "Zinc"), [copper](/wiki/Copper "Copper"), [lead](/wiki/Lead "Lead"), and [tin](/wiki/Tin "Tin") ores. [Landlords](/wiki/Landlords "Landlords") who owned the base metals and coal under their estates had a strong incentive to extract these metals or lease the deposits and collect royalties from mine operators. English, [German](/wiki/Germany "Germany"), and [Dutch](/wiki/Netherlands "Netherlands") [capital](/wiki/Capital_(economics) "Capital (economics)") combined to finance extraction and [refining](/wiki/Refining "Refining"). Hundreds of German [technicians](/wiki/Technicians "Technicians") and skilled workers were brought over; in 1642, a colony of 4,000 foreigners was mining and smelting copper at [Keswick](/wiki/Keswick,_Cumbria "Keswick, Cumbria") in the northwestern mountains.[[21]](#cite_note-21)

Use of water power in the form of [water mills](/wiki/Water_mill "Water mill") was extensive. The water mills were employed to crush ore, raise ore from shafts, and ventilate galleries by powering giant [bellows](/wiki/Bellows "Bellows"). [Black powder](/wiki/Gun_powder "Gun powder") was first used in mining in [Selmecbánya](/wiki/Bansk%C3%A1_%C5%A0tiavnica "Banská Štiavnica"), [Kingdom of Hungary](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Hungary "Kingdom of Hungary") (now [Banská Štiavnica](/wiki/Bansk%C3%A1_%C5%A0tiavnica "Banská Štiavnica"), Slovakia) in 1627.[[22]](#cite_note-22) Black powder allowed blasting of rock and earth to loosen and reveal ore veins. Blasting was much faster than [fire-setting](/wiki/Fire-setting "Fire-setting") and allowed the mining of previously impenetrable metals and ores.[[23]](#cite_note-23) In 1762, one of the world's first mining academies was established in the same town.

The widespread adoption of agricultural innovations such as the iron [plowshare](/wiki/Plowshare "Plowshare"), as well as the growing use of metal as a building material, was also a driving force in the tremendous growth of the iron industry during this period. The Spanish often used inventions such as the [arrastra](/wiki/Arrastra "Arrastra") to pulverize ore after mining. This device was powered by animals and employed the same principles as grain [threshing](/wiki/Threshing "Threshing").[[24]](#cite_note-24)

Much of the knowledge of medieval mining techniques comes from books such as [Biringuccio](/wiki/Biringuccio "Biringuccio")'s *[De la pirotechnia](/wiki/De_la_pirotechnia "De la pirotechnia")* and, probably most importantly, from [Georg Agricola](/wiki/Georg_Agricola "Georg Agricola")'s *[De re metallica](/wiki/De_re_metallica "De re metallica")* (1556). These books detail many different mining methods used in German and Saxon mines. A prime issue in medieval mines, which [Agricola](/wiki/Georgius_Agricola "Georgius Agricola") explains in detail, was the removal of water from mining shafts. As miners dug deeper to access new veins, flooding became a very real obstacle. The mining industry became dramatically more efficient and prosperous with the invention of mechanically- and animal-driven pumps.

### Africa

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mining&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Africa")]

[Iron metallurgy in Africa](/wiki/Iron_metallurgy_in_Africa "Iron metallurgy in Africa") dates back over four thousand years. Gold became an important commodity for Africa during the [trans-Saharan gold trade](/wiki/Trans-Saharan_gold_trade "Trans-Saharan gold trade") from the 7th century to the 14th century. Gold was often traded to Mediterranean economies that demanded gold and could supply [salt](/wiki/Salt "Salt"), even though much of Africa was abundant with salt due to the mines and resources in the [Sahara](/wiki/Sahara_desert "Sahara desert"). The trade of gold for salt was primarily used to promote trade between different economies.[[25]](#cite_note-25) Since the [Great Trek](/wiki/Great_Trek "Great Trek") in the 19th century, after, gold and diamond mining in [Southern Africa](/wiki/Mining_industry_of_South_Africa "Mining industry of South Africa") has had major political and economic impacts. The [Democratic Republic of Congo](/wiki/Democratic_Republic_of_Congo "Democratic Republic of Congo") is the largest producer of diamonds in Africa, with an estimated 12 million carats in 2019. Other types of mining reserves in Africa include [cobalt](/wiki/Cobalt "Cobalt"), [bauxite](/wiki/Bauxite "Bauxite"), [iron ore](/wiki/Iron_ore "Iron ore"), coal, and [copper](/wiki/Copper "Copper").[[26]](#cite_note-26)

### Oceania

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mining&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Oceania")]

Gold and coal mining started in Australia and New Zealand in the 19th century. [Nickel](/wiki/Nickel_mining_in_New_Caledonia "Nickel mining in New Caledonia") has become important in the economy of [New Caledonia](/wiki/New_Caledonia "New Caledonia").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In [Fiji](/wiki/Fiji "Fiji"), in 1934, the Emperor Gold Mining Company Ltd. established operations at [Vatukoula](/wiki/Vatukoula "Vatukoula"), followed in 1935 by the Loloma Gold Mines, N.L., and then by Fiji Mines Development Ltd. (aka Dolphin Mines Ltd.). These developments ushered in a "mining boom", with gold production rising more than a hundred-fold, from 931.4 oz in 1934 to 107,788.5 oz in 1939, an order of magnitude then comparable to the combined output of New Zealand and Australia's eastern states.[[27]](#cite_note-27)

### Americas

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mining&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Americas")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/52/Lead_mining_Barber_1865p321cropped.jpg/250px-Lead_mining_Barber_1865p321cropped.jpg)](/wiki/File:Lead_mining_Barber_1865p321cropped.jpg)

[Lead](/wiki/Lead "Lead") mining in the upper [Mississippi River](/wiki/Mississippi_River "Mississippi River") region of the U.S., 1865

During prehistoric times, early Americans mined large quantities of [copper](/wiki/Copper "Copper") along [Lake Superior](/wiki/Lake_Superior "Lake Superior")'s [Keweenaw Peninsula](/wiki/Keweenaw_Peninsula "Keweenaw Peninsula") and on nearby [Isle Royale](/wiki/Isle_Royale "Isle Royale"); metallic copper remained near the surface into colonial times.[[28]](#cite_note-Lankton91-28)[[29]](#cite_note-west70-29)[[30]](#cite_note-Ricard32-30) Indigenous peoples used Lake Superior copper from at least 5,000 years ago;[[28]](#cite_note-Lankton91-28) copper tools, arrowheads, and other [artifacts](/wiki/Cultural_artifact "Cultural artifact") that were part of an extensive native trade-network have been discovered. In addition, [obsidian](/wiki/Obsidian "Obsidian"), [flint](/wiki/Flint "Flint"), and other minerals were mined, worked, and traded.[[29]](#cite_note-west70-29) Early French explorers who encountered the sites[*[clarification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*] made no use of the metals due to the difficulties of transporting them,[[29]](#cite_note-west70-29) but the copper was eventually[*[when?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Dates_and_numbers#Chronological_items "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Dates and numbers")*] traded throughout the continent along major river routes.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/79/TamarackMiners_CopperCountryMI_sepia.jpg/250px-TamarackMiners_CopperCountryMI_sepia.jpg)](/wiki/File:TamarackMiners_CopperCountryMI_sepia.jpg)

[Miners](/wiki/Miners "Miners") at the [Tamarack Mine](/wiki/Tamarack_mine "Tamarack mine") in [Copper Country](/wiki/Copper_Country "Copper Country"), Michigan, U.S., in 1905

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c9/Mining_factory_-_DPLA_-_cc85a73449447a67878caac4f19a23d2.jpg/250px-Mining_factory_-_DPLA_-_cc85a73449447a67878caac4f19a23d2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mining_factory_-_DPLA_-_cc85a73449447a67878caac4f19a23d2.jpg)

Mining factory, c. 1880–1885. Photographs of the American West, Boston Public Library

In the early colonial history of the Americas, "native gold and silver was quickly expropriated and sent back to Spain in fleets of gold- and silver-laden galleons",[[31]](#cite_note-31) the gold and silver originating mostly from mines in Central and South America. [Turquoise](/wiki/Turquoise "Turquoise") dated at 700 AD was mined in [pre-Columbian](/wiki/Pre-Columbian "Pre-Columbian") America; in the Cerillos Mining District in [New Mexico](/wiki/New_Mexico "New Mexico"), an estimate of "about 15,000 tons of rock had been removed from Mt. Chalchihuitl using [stone tools](/wiki/Stone_tool "Stone tool") before 1700."[[32]](#cite_note-32)[[33]](#cite_note-33)

In 1727 Louis Denys (Denis) (1675–1741), sieur de La Ronde – brother of [Simon-Pierre Denys de Bonaventure](/wiki/Simon-Pierre_Denys_de_Bonaventure "Simon-Pierre Denys de Bonaventure") and the son-in-law of [René Chartier](/wiki/Ren%C3%A9-Louis_Chartier_de_Lotbini%C3%A8re "René-Louis Chartier de Lotbinière") – took command of [Fort La Pointe](/wiki/La_Pointe,_Wisconsin "La Pointe, Wisconsin") at [Chequamegon Bay](/wiki/Chequamegon_Bay "Chequamegon Bay"); where natives informed him of an island of copper. La Ronde obtained permission from the French crown to operate mines in 1733, becoming "the first practical miner on Lake Superior"; seven years later, mining was halted by an outbreak between [Sioux](/wiki/Sioux "Sioux") and [Chippewa](/wiki/Chippewa "Chippewa") tribes.[[34]](#cite_note-34)

[Mining in the United States](/wiki/Mining_in_the_United_States "Mining in the United States") became widespread in the 19th century, and the United States Congress passed the [General Mining Act of 1872](/wiki/General_Mining_Act_of_1872 "General Mining Act of 1872") to encourage mining of federal lands.[[35]](#cite_note-35) As with the [California Gold Rush](/wiki/California_Gold_Rush "California Gold Rush") in the mid-19th century, mining for minerals and precious metals, along with [ranching](/wiki/Ranching "Ranching"), became a driving factor in the U.S. [Westward Expansion](/wiki/Westward_Expansion "Westward Expansion") to the Pacific coast. With the exploration of the West, mining camps sprang up and "expressed a distinctive spirit, an enduring legacy to the new nation"; Gold Rushers would experience the same problems as the Land Rushers of the transient West that preceded them.[[36]](#cite_note-36) Aided by railroads, many people traveled West for work opportunities in mining. Western cities such as [Denver](/wiki/Denver,_Colorado "Denver, Colorado") and [Sacramento](/wiki/Sacramento,_California "Sacramento, California") originated as mining towns.[[37]](#cite_note-37)

When new areas were explored, it was usually the gold ([placer](/wiki/Placer_gold "Placer gold") and then [lode](/wiki/Lode_gold "Lode gold")) and then silver that were taken into possession and extracted first. Other metals would often wait for railroads or canals, as coarse gold dust and nuggets do not require smelting and are easy to identify and transport.[[30]](#cite_note-Ricard32-30)

### Modernity

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mining&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Modernity")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/39/View_showing_miners%E2%80%99_clothes.jpg/250px-View_showing_miners%E2%80%99_clothes.jpg)](/wiki/File:View_showing_miners%E2%80%99_clothes.jpg)

View showing miners' clothes suspended by pulleys, also wash basins and ventilation system, Kirkland Lake, Ontario, 1936

 [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c3/Pyhasalmi_mine.jpg/250px-Pyhasalmi_mine.jpg)](/wiki/File:Pyhasalmi_mine.jpg)

The [Pyhäsalmi Mine](/wiki/Pyh%C3%A4salmi_Mine "Pyhäsalmi Mine"), a metal mine in [Pyhäjärvi](/wiki/Pyh%C3%A4j%C3%A4rvi "Pyhäjärvi"), Finland

In the early 20th century, the gold and silver rush to the western United States also stimulated mining for coal as well as [base metals](/wiki/Base_metal "Base metal") such as copper, lead, and iron. Areas in modern Montana, Utah, Arizona, and later Alaska became major suppliers of copper to the world, which was increasingly in demand for electrical and household goods.[[38]](#cite_note-miller-38) Canada's mining industry grew more slowly than did the United States due to limitations in transportation, capital, and U.S. competition; Ontario was the major producer of the early 20th century with nickel, copper, and gold.[[38]](#cite_note-miller-38)

Meanwhile, Australia experienced the [Australian gold rushes](/wiki/Australian_gold_rushes "Australian gold rushes") and by the 1850s was producing 40% of the world's gold, followed by the establishment of large mines such as the [Mount Morgan Mine](/wiki/Mount_Morgan_Mine "Mount Morgan Mine"), which ran for nearly a hundred years, [Broken Hill ore deposit](/wiki/Broken_Hill_ore_deposit "Broken Hill ore deposit") (one of the largest zinc-lead ore deposits), and the iron ore mines at [Iron Knob](/wiki/Iron_Knob "Iron Knob"). After declines in production, another boom in mining occurred in the 1960s. In the early 21st century, Australia remains a major world mineral producer.[[39]](#cite_note-39)

As the 21st century begins, a globalized [mining industry](#Industry) dominated by large multinational corporations has emerged. [Peak minerals](/wiki/Peak_minerals "Peak minerals") and [environmental impacts](#Environmental_effects) have also become a concern. Different elements, particularly [rare-earth minerals](/wiki/Rare-earth_mineral "Rare-earth mineral"), have begun to increase in demand as a result of new technologies.[[40]](#cite_note-Geosciences_LibreTexts-2017-40)

In 2023, 8.5 billion metric tons of coal were extracted from the Earth's crust. However, as the global economy transitions away from fossil fuels and toward a more sustainable future, the demand for metals is set to skyrocket. Between 2022 and 2050, an estimated 7 billion metric tons of metals will need to be extracted. Steel will account for the largest portion of this total at 5 billion tons, followed by aluminum at 950 million tons, copper at 650 million tons, graphite at 170 million tons, nickel at 100 million tons, and other metals. Notably, the energy expenditure required to extract these metals will soon surpass that of coal mining, highlighting the growing importance of sustainable metal extraction practices.[[41]](#cite_note-41)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d1/Cut_and_fill_schematic.png/500px-Cut_and_fill_schematic.png)](/wiki/File:Cut_and_fill_schematic.png)

Schematic of a [cut and fill mining operation in hard rock](/wiki/Underground_mining_(hard_rock) "Underground mining (hard rock)")

The process of mining, from the discovery of an ore body through mineral extraction to the final return of the land to its natural state, consists of several distinct steps. The first is the discovery of the ore body, carried out through [prospecting](/wiki/Prospecting "Prospecting") or [exploration](/wiki/Mineral_exploration "Mineral exploration") to identify and define its extent, location, and value. This leads to a mathematical [resource estimation](/wiki/Mineral_resource_classification "Mineral resource classification") of the deposit's size and [grade](/wiki/Ore_grade "Ore grade").[[42]](#cite_note-42)

This estimation is used to conduct a pre-feasibility study to determine the theoretical economics of the ore deposit. This identifies early on whether further investment in estimation and engineering studies is warranted and highlights key risks and areas for further work. The next step is to conduct a [feasibility study](/wiki/Mining_feasibility_study "Mining feasibility study") to evaluate the project's financial viability, technical and financial risks, and robustness.[[43]](#cite_note-43)

This is when the mining company decides whether to develop the mine or walk away from the project. This includes mine planning to evaluate the economically recoverable portion of the deposit, the [metallurgy](/wiki/Metallurgy "Metallurgy") and ore recoverability, marketability and payability of the ore concentrates, engineering concerns, milling and infrastructure costs, finance and equity requirements, and an analysis of the proposed mine from the initial excavation all the way through to reclamation. The proportion of a deposit that is economically recoverable is dependent on the [enrichment factor](/wiki/Enrichment_factor "Enrichment factor") of the ore in the area.[[44]](#cite_note-44)

To gain access to the mineral deposit within an area, it is often necessary to mine through or remove [waste material](/wiki/Overburden "Overburden"), which is not of immediate interest to the miner. The total movement of ore and waste constitutes the mining process. Often, more waste than ore is mined during the life of a mine, depending on the nature and location of the ore body. Waste removal and placement are major costs to the mining operator, so a detailed characterization of the waste material forms an essential part of the geological exploration program for a mining operation.[[45]](#cite_note-45)

Once the analysis determines that a given ore body is worth recovering, development begins to provide access to it. The mine buildings and processing plants are built, and any necessary equipment is obtained. The operation of the mine to recover the ore begins and continues as long as the company operating the mine finds it economical to do so. Once all the ore that the mine can produce profitably is recovered, [reclamation](/wiki/Land_rehabilitation "Land rehabilitation") can begin, to make the land used by the mine suitable for future use.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Technical and economic challenges notwithstanding, successful mine development must also address human factors. Working conditions are paramount to success, especially with respect to exposure to dust, radiation, noise, explosive hazards, and vibration, as well as illumination standards. Mining today increasingly must address environmental and community impacts, including psychological and sociological dimensions. Thus, mining educator [Frank T. M. White](/wiki/Frank_T._M._White "Frank T. M. White") (1909–1971) broadened the focus to the "total environment of mining", including reference to community development around mining, and how mining is portrayed to an urban society, which depends on the industry, although seemingly unaware of this dependency. He stated, "[I]n the past, mining engineers have not been called upon to study the psychological, sociological, and personal problems of their own industry – aspects that nowadays are assuming tremendous importance. The mining engineer must rapidly expand his knowledge and his influence into these newer fields."[[46]](#cite_note-46)

## Techniques

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mining&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Techniques")]

Mining techniques can be divided into two common [excavation](/wiki/Earthworks_(engineering) "Earthworks (engineering)") types: [surface mining](/wiki/Surface_mining "Surface mining") and [sub-surface (underground) mining](#Underground_mining). Today, surface mining is much more common and produces, for example, 85% of minerals (excluding petroleum and natural gas) in the United States, including 98% of metallic ores.[[47]](#cite_note-47)

Targets are divided into two general categories of materials: *placer deposits*, consisting of valuable minerals contained within river gravels, beach sands, and other [unconsolidated materials](/wiki/Alluvium "Alluvium"); and *lode deposits*, where valuable minerals are found in veins, in layers, or in mineral grains generally distributed throughout a mass of actual rock. Both types of ore deposit, placer or lode, are mined by both surface and underground methods.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Some mining, including much of the rare earth element and [uranium mining](/wiki/Uranium_mining "Uranium mining"), is done by less common methods, such as [in-situ leaching](/wiki/In-situ_leach "In-situ leach"). This technique involves neither digging at the surface nor underground. The extraction of target minerals by this technique requires that they be soluble, e.g., [potash](/wiki/Potash "Potash"), [potassium chloride](/wiki/Potassium_chloride "Potassium chloride"), [sodium chloride](/wiki/Sodium_chloride "Sodium chloride"), [sodium sulfate](/wiki/Sodium_sulfate "Sodium sulfate"), which dissolve in water. Some minerals, such as copper minerals and [uranium oxide](/wiki/Uranium_oxide "Uranium oxide"), require acid or carbonate solutions to dissolve.[[48]](#cite_note-48)

### Explosives in mining

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mining&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Explosives in mining")]

Explosives have been used in [surface mining](/wiki/Surface_mining "Surface mining") and [sub-surface mining](/wiki/Underground_hard-rock_mining "Underground hard-rock mining") to blast out rock and ore intended for processing. The most common explosive used in mining is [ammonium nitrate](/wiki/Ammonium_nitrate "Ammonium nitrate").[[49]](#cite_note-Bajpayee-2004-49) Between 1870 and 1920, in Queensland, Australia, an increase in mining accidents led to more safety measures surrounding the use of explosives for mining.[[50]](#cite_note-50) In the United States of America, between 1990 and 1999, about 22.3 billion kilograms of explosives were used in mining quarrying and other industries; Moreover "[coal mining](/wiki/Coal_mining "Coal mining") used 66.4%, nonmetal mining and quarrying 13.5%, metal mining 10.4%, [construction](/wiki/Construction "Construction") 7.1%, and all other users 2.6%".[[49]](#cite_note-Bajpayee-2004-49)

### Artisanal

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mining&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Artisanal")]

This section is an excerpt from [Artisanal mining](/wiki/Artisanal_mining "Artisanal mining").[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Artisanal_mining&action=edit)]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d1/Wolframite_Mining_in_Kailo%2C_DRC.jpg/330px-Wolframite_Mining_in_Kailo%2C_DRC.jpg)](/wiki/File:Wolframite_Mining_in_Kailo,_DRC.jpg)

Artisanal [wolframite](/wiki/Wolframite "Wolframite") mining in [Kailo Territory](/wiki/Kailo_Territory "Kailo Territory"), DRC

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/06/Artisanal_gold_Dodoma001.jpg/250px-Artisanal_gold_Dodoma001.jpg)](/wiki/File:Artisanal_gold_Dodoma001.jpg)

Artisanal gold mines near [Dodoma](/wiki/Dodoma "Dodoma"), Tanzania. Makeshift sails lead fresh air underground.

[Artisanal and small-scale mining](/wiki/Artisanal_mining "Artisanal mining") (ASM) is a blanket term for a wide variety of types of small mining that range from manual subsistence mining using simple tools to vocational mining that is semi-mechanised involving light machinery such as [generators](/wiki/Electric_generator "Electric generator"), water [pumps](/wiki/Pump "Pump"), and small motorized mills, through to organised mechanised mining that employs industrial equipment such as [excavators](/wiki/Excavator "Excavator") and [bulldozers](/wiki/Bulldozer "Bulldozer"). ASM involves miners who may or may not be officially employed. Although there can be large numbers of miners working at a mining site, they typically work in small teams according to a customary system of organisation that includes a manager, skilled and unskilled labour.

While the terms are generally used interchangeably or synonymously, by definition 'artisanal mining' refers to purely manual labor while 'small-scale mining' typically involves larger operations and some use of mechanical or industrial tools.[[51]](#cite_note-51) While there is no completely coherent definition for ASM, artisanal mining generally includes miners who are not officially employed by a mining company and use their own resources to mine. As such, they are part of an [informal economy](/wiki/Informal_economy "Informal economy"). ASM also includes, in small-scale mining, enterprises or individuals that employ workers for mining, but who generally still use similar manually-intensive methods as artisanal miners (such as working with hand tools). In addition, ASM can be characterized as distinct from large-scale mining (LSM) by less efficient extraction of pure [minerals](/wiki/Mineral "Mineral") from the [ore](/wiki/Ore "Ore"), lower wages, decreased occupational safety, benefits, and health standards for miners, and a lack of [environmental protection](/wiki/Environmental_impact_of_mining "Environmental impact of mining") measures.[[52]](#cite_note-Artisanal_mining_:42-52) ASM has on occasion been evaluated positively in terms of negligible [capital outflow](/wiki/Capital_outflow "Capital outflow"), the employment it generates and the connection it has with local society and economy in contrast with the [enclave economies](/wiki/Enclave_economy "Enclave economy") of some LSM.[[53]](#cite_note-53)

Artisanal miners often undertake the activity of mining seasonally. For example, [crops](/wiki/Crop "Crop") are planted in the [rainy season](/wiki/Rainy_season "Rainy season"), and mining is pursued in the [dry season](/wiki/Dry_season "Dry season"). However, they also frequently travel to mining areas and work year-round. There are four broad types of ASM:[[54]](#cite_note-54)

1. Permanent artisanal mining
2. Seasonal (annually migrating during idle agriculture periods)
3. Rush-type (massive migration, pulled often by commodity price jumps)
4. Shock-push (poverty-driven, following conflict or natural disasters).

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b7/Artisanal_mine_interior_near_Lows_Creek_Mpumalanga_01.jpg/250px-Artisanal_mine_interior_near_Lows_Creek_Mpumalanga_01.jpg)](/wiki/File:Artisanal_mine_interior_near_Lows_Creek_Mpumalanga_01.jpg)

Interior of an artisanal mine near [Low's Creek](/w/index.php?title=Low%27s_Creek&action=edit&redlink=1 "Low's Creek (page does not exist)"), [Mpumalanga Province](/wiki/Mpumalanga_Province "Mpumalanga Province"), South Africa. The human figures, exploring this mine, show the scale of tunnels driven entirely with hand tools (two-kilogram (4.4 lb) hammer and hand-forged scrap-steel chisel).

ASM is an important socio-economic sector for the rural poor in many developing nations, many of whom have few other options for supporting their families. Over 90% of the world's mining workforce are engaged in ASM, with an estimated 40.5 million people directly engaged in ASM, from over 80 countries in the [global south](/wiki/Global_south "Global south"). More than 150 million people indirectly depend on ASM for their livelihood. 70–80% of small-scale miners are informal, and approximately 30% are women, although this ranges in certain countries and commodities from 5% to 80%.[[55]](#cite_note-55)

### Surface

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mining&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Surface")]

Main article: [Surface mining](/wiki/Surface_mining "Surface mining")

[Surface mining](/wiki/Surface_mining "Surface mining") is done by removing surface vegetation, dirt, and bedrock to reach buried ore deposits. Techniques of surface mining include: [open-pit mining](/wiki/Open-pit_mining "Open-pit mining"), which is the recovery of materials from an open pit in the ground; [quarrying](/wiki/Quarry "Quarry"), identical to open-pit mining except that it refers to sand, stone and clay; [strip mining](/wiki/Strip_mining "Strip mining"), which consists of stripping surface layers off to reveal ore underneath; and [mountaintop removal](/wiki/Mountaintop_removal_mining "Mountaintop removal mining"), commonly associated with coal mining, which involves taking the top of a mountain off to reach ore deposits at depth. Most placer deposits, because they are shallowly buried, are mined by surface methods. Finally, [landfill mining](/wiki/Landfill_mining "Landfill mining") involves excavating and processing [landfill](/wiki/Landfill "Landfill") sites.[[56]](#cite_note-56) Landfill mining has been thought of as a long-term solution to [methane emissions](/wiki/Methane_emissions "Methane emissions") and local pollution.[[57]](#cite_note-57)

### High wall

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mining&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: High wall")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f1/Mega_HIghwall_Mining_ADDCAR_16.jpg/250px-Mega_HIghwall_Mining_ADDCAR_16.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mega_HIghwall_Mining_ADDCAR_16.jpg)

Coalburg Seam highwall mining at ADDCAR 16 Logan County WV

High-wall mining, which evolved from auger mining, is another form of surface mining. In high wall mining, the remaining part of a coal seam previously exploited by other surface-mining techniques has too much overburden to be removed but can still be profitably exploited from the side of the artificial cliff made by previous mining.[[58]](#cite_note-58) A typical cycle alternates sumping, which undercuts the seam, and shearing, which raises and lowers the cutter-head boom to cut the entire height of the coal seam. As the coal recovery cycle continues, the cutter head is progressively advanced further into the coal seam. High wall mining can produce thousands of tons of coal in contour-strip operations with narrow benches, previously mined areas, trench mine applications, and steep-dip seams.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]
