### Underground mining

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mining&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Underground mining")]

Main articles: [Underground mining (hard rock)](/wiki/Underground_mining_(hard_rock) "Underground mining (hard rock)") and [Underground mining (soft rock)](/wiki/Underground_mining_(soft_rock) "Underground mining (soft rock)")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b2/Kilianstollen_Zug.jpg/250px-Kilianstollen_Zug.jpg)](/wiki/File:Kilianstollen_Zug.jpg)

[Mantrip](/wiki/Mantrip "Mantrip") used for transporting miners within an underground mine

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9c/Caterpillar_Highwall_Mine_HW300.jpg/250px-Caterpillar_Highwall_Mine_HW300.jpg)](/wiki/File:Caterpillar_Highwall_Mine_HW300.jpg)

Caterpillar Highwall Miner HW300 – Technology Bridging Underground and Open Pit Mining

Subsurface mining involves digging tunnels or shafts into the earth to access buried ore deposits. Ore for processing and waste rock for disposal are brought to the surface through the tunnels and shafts. Subsurface mining can be classified by the type of access shafts used, the extraction method, or the technique for reaching the mineral deposit. [Drift mining](/wiki/Drift_mining "Drift mining") uses horizontal access tunnels, [slope mining](/wiki/Slope_mining "Slope mining") uses diagonally sloping access shafts, and [shaft mining](/wiki/Shaft_mining "Shaft mining") uses vertical access shafts. Mining in [hard](/wiki/Underground_mining_(hard_rock) "Underground mining (hard rock)") and [soft](/wiki/Underground_mining_(soft_rock) "Underground mining (soft rock)") rock formations requires different techniques.[[59]](#cite_note-59)

Other methods include [shrinkage stope mining](/wiki/Shrinkage_stope_mining "Shrinkage stope mining"), which is mining upward, creating a sloping underground room, [long wall mining](/wiki/Long_wall_mining "Long wall mining"), which is grinding a long ore surface underground, and [room and pillar](/wiki/Room_and_pillar "Room and pillar") mining, which is removing ore from rooms while leaving pillars in place to support the roof of the room. Room-and-pillar mining often leads to [retreat mining](/wiki/Retreat_mining "Retreat mining"), in which supporting pillars are removed as miners retreat, allowing the room to cave in and thereby loosening more ore. Additional sub-surface mining methods include [hard rock mining](/wiki/Underground_mining_(hard_rock) "Underground mining (hard rock)"), borehole mining, drift and fill mining, long hole slope mining, sub-level caving, and [block caving](/wiki/Block_caving "Block caving").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## Machines

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mining&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Machines")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6a/Bagger-garzweiler.jpg/500px-Bagger-garzweiler.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bagger-garzweiler.jpg)

The [Bagger 288](/wiki/Bagger_288 "Bagger 288") is a [bucket-wheel excavator](/wiki/Bucket-wheel_excavator "Bucket-wheel excavator") used in [strip mining](/wiki/Strip_mining "Strip mining"). It is also one of the largest land vehicles of all time.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/54/Coal_Haul_Truck_at_North_Antelope_Rochelle.png/250px-Coal_Haul_Truck_at_North_Antelope_Rochelle.png)](/wiki/File:Coal_Haul_Truck_at_North_Antelope_Rochelle.png)

A Bucyrus Erie 2570 dragline and [CAT 797](/wiki/Caterpillar_797 "Caterpillar 797") haul truck at the North Antelope Rochelle opencut coal mine

[Heavy machinery](/wiki/Heavy_machinery "Heavy machinery") is used in mining to explore and develop sites, to remove and stockpile overburden, to break and remove rocks of various hardness and toughness, to process the ore, and to carry out reclamation projects after the mine is closed. Bulldozers, drills, explosives, and trucks are all necessary for excavating the land. In the case of [placer mining](/wiki/Placer_mining "Placer mining"), unconsolidated gravel, or [alluvium](/wiki/Alluvium "Alluvium"), is fed into machinery consisting of a hopper and a shaking screen or [trommel](/wiki/Trommel "Trommel"), which frees the desired minerals from the waste gravel. The minerals are then concentrated using [sluices](/wiki/Sluices "Sluices") or jigs.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Large [drills](/wiki/Diamond_drilling "Diamond drilling") are used to sink shafts, excavate stopes, and obtain samples for analysis, and to extract natural gas (see [Fracking](/wiki/Fracking "Fracking")). [Trams](/wiki/Tram "Tram") are used to transport miners, minerals, and waste. Lifts carry miners into and out of mines, move rock and ore out, and move machinery in and out of underground mines. Huge trucks, shovels, and cranes are employed in surface mining to move large quantities of overburden and ore. Processing plants use large crushers, mills, reactors, roasters, and other equipment to consolidate the mineral-rich material and extract the desired compounds and metals from the ore.[[60]](#cite_note-60)

## Processing

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mining&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Processing")]

Main articles: [Mineral processing](/wiki/Mineral_processing "Mineral processing") and [Extractive metallurgy](/wiki/Extractive_metallurgy "Extractive metallurgy")

Once the mineral is extracted, it is often then processed. The science of [extractive metallurgy](/wiki/Extractive_metallurgy "Extractive metallurgy") is a specialized branch of metallurgy that studies the extraction of valuable metals from ores, particularly by chemical or mechanical means.[[61]](#cite_note-61)[[62]](#cite_note-62)

[Mineral processing](/wiki/Mineral_processing "Mineral processing") (or mineral dressing) is a specialized area in the science of metallurgy that studies the mechanical means of crushing, grinding, and washing that enable the separation (extractive metallurgy) of valuable metals or minerals from their [gangue](/wiki/Gangue "Gangue") (waste material). Processing of placer ore material consists of gravity-based separation methods, such as [Sluice boxes](/wiki/Sluice_box "Sluice box"). Only minor shaking or washing may be necessary to disaggregate (unclump) the sands or gravels before processing. Processing ore from a lode mine, whether surface or subsurface, requires crushing and pulverizing the ore before extraction of the valuable minerals begins. After the ore is crushed, the valuable minerals are recovered using one or a combination of mechanical and chemical techniques.[[63]](#cite_note-63)

Since most metals are present in ores as oxides or sulfides, the metal needs to be [reduced](/wiki/Redox "Redox") to its metallic form. This can be accomplished through chemical means such as [smelting](/wiki/Smelting "Smelting") or through electrolytic reduction, as in the case of [aluminium](/wiki/Aluminium "Aluminium"). [Geometallurgy](/wiki/Geometallurgy "Geometallurgy") combines the geologic sciences with extractive metallurgy and mining.[[40]](#cite_note-Geosciences_LibreTexts-2017-40)

In 2018, led by Chemistry and Biochemistry professor Bradley D. Smith, [University of Notre Dame](/wiki/University_of_Notre_Dame "University of Notre Dame") researchers "invented a new class of molecules whose shape and size enable them to capture and contain precious metal ions," reported in a study published by the [Journal of the American Chemical Society](/wiki/Journal_of_the_American_Chemical_Society "Journal of the American Chemical Society"). The new method "converts gold-containing ore into [chloroauric acid](/wiki/Chloroauric_acid "Chloroauric acid") and extracts it using an industrial solvent. The container molecules are able to selectively separate the gold from the solvent without the use of water stripping." The newly developed molecules can eliminate water stripping, whereas mining traditionally "relies on a 125-year-old method that treats gold-containing ore with large quantities of poisonous [sodium cyanide](/wiki/Sodium_cyanide "Sodium cyanide")... this new process has a milder environmental impact and that, besides gold, it can be used for capturing other metals such as platinum and palladium," and could also be used in urban mining processes that remove precious metals from wastewater streams.[[64]](#cite_note-64)

## Industry

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mining&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Industry")]

Main pages: [List of mines](/wiki/List_of_mines "List of mines"), [List of mining companies](/wiki/List_of_mining_companies "List of mining companies"), [Category:Mining companies](/wiki/Category:Mining_companies "Category:Mining companies"), and [Category:Mining by country](/wiki/Category:Mining_by_country "Category:Mining by country")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1c/Siilinj%C3%A4rvi_S%C3%A4rkij%C3%A4rvi_pit.jpg/250px-Siilinj%C3%A4rvi_S%C3%A4rkij%C3%A4rvi_pit.jpg)](/wiki/File:Siilinj%C3%A4rvi_S%C3%A4rkij%C3%A4rvi_pit.jpg)

The Särkijärvi pit of the [apatite mine](/wiki/Siilinj%C3%A4rvi_carbonatite "Siilinjärvi carbonatite") in [Siilinjärvi](/wiki/Siilinj%C3%A4rvi "Siilinjärvi"), [Finland](/wiki/Finland "Finland")

Mining exists in many countries. [London](/wiki/London "London") is the headquarters of [Anglo American](/wiki/Anglo_American_plc "Anglo American plc"). [Melbourne](/wiki/Melbourne "Melbourne") is the headquarters of the world's two largest mining companies [BHP](/wiki/BHP "BHP") and [Rio Tinto](/wiki/Rio_Tinto_(corporation) "Rio Tinto (corporation)").[[65]](#cite_note-NAIndustryProfile-65) The US mining industry is also large and dominated by extraction of coal and other nonmetal minerals (e.g., rock and sand), and various regulations have worked to reduce the significance of mining in the United States.[[65]](#cite_note-NAIndustryProfile-65) In 2007, the total [market capitalization](/wiki/Market_capitalization "Market capitalization") of mining companies was reported at US$962 billion, which compares to a total global market cap of publicly traded companies of about US$50 trillion in 2007.[[66]](#cite_note-66) In 2002, Chile and Peru were reportedly the major mining countries of [South America](/wiki/South_America "South America").[[67]](#cite_note-67) The [mineral industry of Africa](/wiki/Mineral_industry_of_Africa "Mineral industry of Africa") includes the mining of various minerals; it produces relatively little of the industrial metals [copper](/wiki/Copper "Copper"), [lead](/wiki/Lead "Lead"), and [zinc](/wiki/Zinc "Zinc"), but according to one estimate has as a percent of world reserves 40% of [gold](/wiki/Gold "Gold"), 60% of [cobalt](/wiki/Cobalt "Cobalt"), and 90% of the world's [platinum group](/wiki/Platinum_group "Platinum group") metals.[[68]](#cite_note-68) [Mining in India](/wiki/Mining_in_India "Mining in India") is a significant part of that country's economy. In the developed world, [mining in Australia](/wiki/Mining_in_Australia "Mining in Australia"), with BHP founded and headquartered in the country, and mining in Canada, are particularly significant. For [rare earth minerals](/wiki/Rare_earth_mineral "Rare earth mineral") mining, [China](/wiki/China "China") reportedly controlled 95% of production in 2013.[[69]](#cite_note-69)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/54/Bingham_Canyon_April_2005.jpg/250px-Bingham_Canyon_April_2005.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bingham_Canyon_April_2005.jpg)

The [Bingham Canyon Mine](/wiki/Bingham_Canyon_Mine "Bingham Canyon Mine") of Rio Tinto's subsidiary, Kennecott Utah Copper

While individual entrepreneurs or small businesses can conduct exploration and mining, most modern-day mines are large enterprises that require substantial capital to establish. Consequently, the mining sector of the industry is dominated by large, often multinational companies, most of which are [publicly listed](/wiki/Publicly_listed "Publicly listed"). It can be argued that what is referred to as the 'mining industry' comprises two sectors: one specializing in the exploration of new resources and the other in mining those resources. The exploration sector is typically made up of individuals and small mineral resource companies, called "juniors", that depend on [venture capital](/wiki/Venture_capital "Venture capital"). The mining sector comprises large multinational companies that are sustained by production from their operations. Various other industries, such as equipment manufacturing, environmental testing, and metallurgical analysis, rely on and support the mining industry worldwide. Canadian stock exchanges have a particular focus on mining companies, particularly junior exploration companies, through Toronto's [TSX Venture Exchange](/wiki/TSX_Venture_Exchange "TSX Venture Exchange"); Canadian companies raise capital on these exchanges and then invest the money in exploration globally.[[65]](#cite_note-NAIndustryProfile-65) Some have argued that below juniors, there exists a substantial sector of illegitimate companies primarily focused on manipulating stock prices.[[65]](#cite_note-NAIndustryProfile-65)

Mining operations can be grouped into five major categories based on the resources they exploit. These are [oil and gas extraction](/wiki/Extraction_of_petroleum "Extraction of petroleum"), [coal mining](/wiki/Coal_mining "Coal mining"), metal ore mining, nonmetallic mineral mining and quarrying, and mining support activities.[[70]](#cite_note-70) Of all of these categories, [oil and gas extraction](/wiki/Oil_and_gas_extraction "Oil and gas extraction") remains one of the largest in terms of its global economic importance. Prospecting potential mining sites, a vital area of concern for the mining industry, is now done using sophisticated new technologies such as seismic prospecting and [remote-sensing satellites](/wiki/Remote_sensing "Remote sensing"). Mining is heavily affected by the prices of the [commodity](/wiki/Commodity "Commodity") minerals, which are often volatile. The [2000s commodities boom](/wiki/2000s_commodities_boom "2000s commodities boom") ("commodities supercycle") increased commodity prices, driving aggressive mining. In addition, the price of gold increased dramatically in the 2000s, which increased [gold mining](/wiki/Gold_mining "Gold mining"); for example, one study found that conversion of forest in the Amazon increased six-fold from the period 2003–2006 (292 ha/yr) to the period 2006–2009 (1,915 ha/yr), largely due to artisanal mining.[[71]](#cite_note-71)

### Corporate classifications

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mining&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Corporate classifications")]

Mining companies can be classified based on their size and financial capabilities:

* *Major* companies are considered to have an adjusted annual mining-related revenue of more than US$500 million, with the financial capability to develop a major mine on its own.
* *Intermediate* companies have at least $50 million in annual revenue but less than $500 million.
* *Junior* companies rely on [equity](/wiki/Equity_(economics) "Equity (economics)") financing as their principal means of funding exploration. Juniors are mainly pure exploration companies, but may also produce minimally, and do not have a revenue exceeding US$50 million.[[72]](#cite_note-MEG-CorpClass-72)

Regarding their valuation and stock market characteristics, see [Valuation (finance) § Valuation of mining projects](/wiki/Valuation_(finance)#Valuation_of_mining_projects "Valuation (finance)").

### World Bank

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mining&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: World Bank")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/86/World_Bank_logo.svg/250px-World_Bank_logo.svg.png)](/wiki/File:World_Bank_logo.svg)

World Bank logo

The [World Bank](/wiki/World_Bank "World Bank") has been involved in mining since 1955, mainly through grants from its [International Bank for Reconstruction and Development](/wiki/International_Bank_for_Reconstruction_and_Development "International Bank for Reconstruction and Development"), with the Bank's [Multilateral Investment Guarantee Agency](/wiki/Multilateral_Investment_Guarantee_Agency "Multilateral Investment Guarantee Agency") offering [political risk](/wiki/Political_risk "Political risk") insurance.[[73]](#cite_note-73) Between 1955 and 1990 it provided about $2 billion to fifty mining projects, broadly categorized as reform and rehabilitation, greenfield mine construction, mineral processing, technical assistance, and engineering. These projects have been criticized, particularly the [Ferro Carajas project](/wiki/Caraj%C3%A1s_Mine "Carajás Mine") of Brazil, begun in 1981.[[74]](#cite_note-74) The World Bank established mining codes intended to increase foreign investment; in 1988, it solicited feedback from 45 mining companies on how to increase their involvement.[[75]](#cite_note-RocksHardPlaces-75): 20

In 1992, the World Bank began to push for privatization of [government-owned mining companies](/wiki/Government-owned_corporation "Government-owned corporation") with a new set of codes, beginning with its report *The Strategy for African Mining*. In 1997, Latin America's largest miner [Companhia Vale do Rio Doce](/wiki/Vale_(mining_company) "Vale (mining company)") (CVRD) was privatized. These and other developments, such as the Philippines 1995 Mining Act, led the bank to publish a third report (*Assistance for Minerals Sector Development and Reform in Member Countries*), which endorsed mandatory environmental impact assessments and attention to the concerns of the local population. The codes based on this report are influential in the legislation of developing nations. The new codes are intended to encourage development through tax holidays, zero customs duties, reduced income taxes, and related measures.[[75]](#cite_note-RocksHardPlaces-75): 22 The results of these codes were analyzed by a group from the University of Quebec, which concluded that the codes promote foreign investment but "fall very short of permitting sustainable development".[[76]](#cite_note-76) The observed negative correlation between natural resources and economic development is known as the [resource curse](/wiki/Resource_curse "Resource curse").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## Safety

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mining&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Safety")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cf/Transporte_por_cable_de_minerales%2C_Devnya%2C_Bulgaria%2C_2016-05-27%2C_DD_65.jpg/250px-Transporte_por_cable_de_minerales%2C_Devnya%2C_Bulgaria%2C_2016-05-27%2C_DD_65.jpg)](/wiki/File:Transporte_por_cable_de_minerales,_Devnya,_Bulgaria,_2016-05-27,_DD_65.jpg)

Mining transport in Devnya, Bulgaria

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ea/Coal_miner_spraying_rock_dust.jpg/250px-Coal_miner_spraying_rock_dust.jpg)](/wiki/File:Coal_miner_spraying_rock_dust.jpg)

A coal miner in [West Virginia](/wiki/West_Virginia "West Virginia") spraying rock dust to reduce the combustible fraction of coal dust in the air

Main article: [Mine safety](/wiki/Mine_safety "Mine safety")

Safety has long been a concern in the mining business, especially in sub-surface mining. The [Courrières mine disaster](/wiki/Courri%C3%A8res_mine_disaster "Courrières mine disaster"), Europe's worst [mining accident](/wiki/Mining_accident "Mining accident"), involved the death of 1,099 miners in Northern [France](/wiki/France "France") on March 10, 1906. This disaster was surpassed only by the [Benxihu Colliery](/wiki/Benxihu_Colliery "Benxihu Colliery") accident in [China](/wiki/China "China") on April 26, 1942, which killed 1,549 miners.[[77]](#cite_note-77) Although mining today is substantially safer than it was in previous decades, [mining accidents](/wiki/Mining_accident "Mining accident") still occur.

Government figures indicate that around 5,000 Chinese miners die in accidents each year, while other reports have suggested a figure as high as 20,000.[[78]](#cite_note-78)

Between 1870 and 1920 in Queensland, Australia, an increase in mining accidents led to more safety measures regarding the use of explosives in mining.[[79]](#cite_note-79)

In the Democratic Republic of the Congo (DRC), safety concerns have been raised for both those who work in the mines and those who live in the surrounding areas. Workers are usually seen with a mustard-colored powder that turned out to be "...dried sulfuric acid, [which] they use in the mine to process the [mineral] ores.[[80]](#cite_note-80) Surrounding the mine, there is a "small stream of foul, sludgy water that passes beneath a bridge next to the fence."[[81]](#cite_note-:1-81) Around the mine, biomonitoring studies of metal exposure show that "The levels found in Kinshasa remain above the CDC reference value (>5 μg/dL) and constitute a public health concern," and that subjects living close (< 3 km) to mines or metal-processing industries have higher urinary concentrations of various metals (p < 0.01) than those living further away (3-10 km)," revealing an "... absence of a national policy to control and prevent exposure to harmful chemicals."[[82]](#cite_note-82) In the mines, specifically at the Tenke Fungurume mine, there were hundreds of thousands of people [who] engaged in the feverish excavation of cobalt in medieval conditions." These workers were locals who worked for "two dollars (roughly one day's income)."[[81]](#cite_note-:1-81) In addition, the rates of schistosomiasis, a mining related disease endemic to DRC, were found to be "higher among mining workers (27%) than in the total population (<13 %),"[[83]](#cite_note-83) indicating harmful long-term impacts for the workers.

In South Africa, issues are similar to those in the DRC. Miners are exposed to silica dust, which "increases the risk of pulmonary tuberculosis,"[[84]](#cite_note-84) with a tuberculosis rate of "over 1,000 per 100,000."[[85]](#cite_note-:2-85) "Mine shafts themselves are crowded and poorly-ventilated, but so are hostels where over a dozen men can share a small room. These conditions are highly conducive to infection; the rate of recurrent tuberculosis in a recent South African prospective cohort of 600 miners was about 8 per 100 person-years," which increases the risk of tuberculosis spreading in both mining and living locations. Past the mines, the "extraction of minerals often leads to the disruption of ecosystems, polluting water sources, and degrading habitats including cultural heritage critical for the livelihoods of people."[[85]](#cite_note-:2-85)

Mining accidents continue worldwide, including incidents that have caused dozens of fatalities at a time, such as the 2007 [Ulyanovskaya Mine disaster](/wiki/Ulyanovskaya_Mine_disaster "Ulyanovskaya Mine disaster") in Russia, the [2009 Heilongjiang mine explosion](/wiki/2009_Heilongjiang_mine_explosion "2009 Heilongjiang mine explosion") in China, and the 2010 [Upper Big Branch Mine disaster](/wiki/Upper_Big_Branch_Mine_disaster "Upper Big Branch Mine disaster") in the United States. Mining has been identified by the [National Institute for Occupational Safety and Health](/wiki/National_Institute_for_Occupational_Safety_and_Health "National Institute for Occupational Safety and Health") (NIOSH) as a priority industry sector in the [National Occupational Research Agenda](/wiki/National_Occupational_Research_Agenda "National Occupational Research Agenda") (NORA) to identify and provide intervention strategies regarding occupational health and safety issues.[[86]](#cite_note-86) The [Mining Safety and Health Administration](/wiki/Mine_Safety_and_Health_Administration "Mine Safety and Health Administration") (MSHA) was established in 1978 to "work to prevent death, illness, and injury from mining and promote safe and healthful workplaces for US miners."[[87]](#cite_note-87) Since its implementation in 1978, the number of miner fatalities has decreased from 242 miners in 1978 to 24 miners in 2019.[[88]](#cite_note-88)

There are numerous [occupational hazards](/wiki/Occupational_hazard "Occupational hazard") associated with mining, including exposure to [rockdust](/wiki/Rockdust "Rockdust"), which can lead to diseases such as [silicosis](/wiki/Silicosis "Silicosis"), [asbestosis](/wiki/Asbestosis "Asbestosis"), and [pneumoconiosis](/wiki/Pneumoconiosis "Pneumoconiosis"). Gases in the mine can lead to [asphyxiation](/wiki/Asphyxia "Asphyxia") and have a risk of ignition. Mining equipment can generate considerable noise, putting workers at risk for [hearing loss](/wiki/Hearing_loss "Hearing loss"). [Cave-ins](/wiki/Cave-in "Cave-in"), [rock falls](/wiki/Rockfall "Rockfall"), and exposure to excess heat are also known hazards. The current NIOSH Recommended Exposure Limit (REL) of noise is 85 dBA with a 3 dBA exchange rate, and the MSHA Permissible Exposure Limit (PEL) is 90 dBA with a 5 dBA exchange rate as an 8-hour time-weighted average. NIOSH has found that 25% of noise-exposed workers in Mining, Quarrying, and Oil and Gas Extraction have hearing impairment.[[89]](#cite_note-89) The prevalence of hearing loss increased by 1% from 1991 to 2001 within these workers.[[90]](#cite_note-90)

Noise studies have been conducted in several mining environments. Stageloaders (84-102 dBA), shearers (85-99 dBA), auxiliary fans (84–120 dBA), continuous mining machines (78–109 dBA), and roof bolters (92–103 dBA) represent some of the noisiest equipment in underground [coal mines](/wiki/Coal_mining "Coal mining").[[91]](#cite_note-91) [Dragline](/wiki/Dragline_excavator "Dragline excavator") oilers, dozer operators, and welders using air arcing were occupations with the highest noise exposures among surface coal miners.[[92]](#cite_note-92) Coal mines had the highest hearing loss injury likelihood.[[93]](#cite_note-93)

## Environmental effects

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mining&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Environmental effects")]

This section is an excerpt from [Environmental impact of mining](/wiki/Environmental_impact_of_mining "Environmental impact of mining").[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Environmental_impact_of_mining&action=edit)]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b0/Rio_tinto_river_CarolStoker_NASA_Ames_Research_Center.jpg/250px-Rio_tinto_river_CarolStoker_NASA_Ames_Research_Center.jpg)](/wiki/File:Rio_tinto_river_CarolStoker_NASA_Ames_Research_Center.jpg)

[Acid mine drainage](/wiki/Acid_mine_drainage "Acid mine drainage") in the [Rio Tinto River](/wiki/Rio_Tinto_River "Rio Tinto River")

[Environmental impact of mining](/wiki/Environmental_impact_of_mining "Environmental impact of mining") can occur at local, regional, and global scales through direct and indirect mining practices. Mining can cause [erosion](/wiki/Erosion "Erosion"), [sinkholes](/wiki/Sinkhole "Sinkhole"), [loss of biodiversity](/wiki/Biodiversity_loss "Biodiversity loss"), or the [contamination of soil](/wiki/Soil_contamination "Soil contamination"), [groundwater](/wiki/Groundwater_pollution "Groundwater pollution"), and [surface water](/wiki/Surface_water "Surface water") by chemicals emitted from mining processes. These processes also affect the atmosphere through carbon emissions which contributes to [climate change](/wiki/Climate_change "Climate change").[[94]](#cite_note-94)

Some mining methods ([lithium](/wiki/Lithium "Lithium") mining, [phosphate](/wiki/Phosphate_mineral "Phosphate mineral") mining, [coal](/wiki/Coal "Coal") mining, [mountaintop removal mining](/wiki/Mountaintop_removal_mining "Mountaintop removal mining"), and [sand](/wiki/Sand "Sand") mining) may have such significant environmental and public health effects[[95]](#cite_note-95) that mining companies in some countries are required to follow strict environmental and rehabilitation codes to ensure that the mined area returns to its original state. Mining can provide various advantages to societies, yet it can also spark conflicts, particularly regarding land use both above and below the surface.[[96]](#cite_note-96)

Mining operations remain rigorous and intrusive, often resulting in significant environmental impacts on local ecosystems and broader implications for planetary environmental health.[[97]](#cite_note-97) To accommodate mines and associated infrastructure, land is cleared extensively, consuming significant energy and water resources, emitting air pollutants, and producing [hazardous waste](/wiki/Hazardous_waste "Hazardous waste").[[98]](#cite_note-98)

According to The World Counts page "The amount of resources mined from Earth is up from 39.3 billion tons in 2002. A 55 percent increase in less than 20 years. This puts Earth's natural resources under heavy pressure. We are already extracting 75 percent more than Earth can sustain in the long run."[[99]](#cite_note-99)

### Environmental regulation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mining&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Environmental regulation")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b8/Iron_hydroxide_precipitate_in_stream.jpg/250px-Iron_hydroxide_precipitate_in_stream.jpg)](/wiki/File:Iron_hydroxide_precipitate_in_stream.jpg)

Iron hydroxide precipitate stains a stream receiving acid drainage from surface coal mining.

Countries with strongly enforced mining regulations commonly require [environmental impact assessment](/wiki/Environmental_impact_assessment "Environmental impact assessment"), development of [environmental management](/wiki/Environmental_management "Environmental management") plans, and [mine closure planning](/wiki/Mine_closure_planning "Mine closure planning") before beginning mine operations. [Environmental monitoring](/wiki/Environmental_monitoring "Environmental monitoring") during operation and after closure may also be required. Government regulations may not be well enforced, especially in the developing world.[[40]](#cite_note-Geosciences_LibreTexts-2017-40)

For major mining companies and any company seeking international financing, there are some other mechanisms to enforce environmental standards. These generally relate to financing standards such as the [Equator Principles](/wiki/Equator_Principles "Equator Principles"), [IFC](/wiki/International_Finance_Corporation "International Finance Corporation") environmental standards, and criteria for [Socially responsible investing](/wiki/Socially_responsible_investing "Socially responsible investing"). Mining companies have used this oversight from the financial sector to argue for some level of [industry self-regulation](/wiki/Industry_self-regulation "Industry self-regulation").[[75]](#cite_note-RocksHardPlaces-75) In 1992, a Draft Code of Conduct for Transnational Corporations was proposed at the [Rio Earth Summit](/wiki/Earth_Summit_(1992) "Earth Summit (1992)") by the UN Centre for Transnational Corporations (UNCTC), but the Business Council for Sustainable Development (BCSD) together with the International Chamber of Commerce (ICC) argued successfully for self-regulation instead.[[100]](#cite_note-100)

This was followed by the Global Mining Initiative which was begun by nine of the largest metals and mining companies and which led to the formation of the [International Council on Mining and Metals](/wiki/International_Council_on_Mining_and_Metals "International Council on Mining and Metals"), whose purpose was to "act as a catalyst" in an effort to improve social and environmental performance in the mining and metals industry internationally.[[75]](#cite_note-RocksHardPlaces-75) The mining industry has provided funding to various conservation groups, some of which have been working with conservation agendas that are at odds with an emerging acceptance of the rights of indigenous people – particularly the right to make land-use decisions.[[101]](#cite_note-101)

Certification of [mines with good practices](/wiki/Mineral_industry_of_Peru "Mineral industry of Peru") is issued by the [International Organization for Standardization](/wiki/International_Organization_for_Standardization "International Organization for Standardization") (ISO). For example, [ISO 9000](/wiki/ISO_9000 "ISO 9000") and [ISO 14001](/wiki/ISO_14001 "ISO 14001"), which certify an "auditable environmental management system", involve brief inspections, although they have been criticized for lacking rigor.[*[clarification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*][[75]](#cite_note-RocksHardPlaces-75): 183–84 Certification is also available through [Ceres](/wiki/Ceres_(organization) "Ceres (organization)")' [Global Reporting Initiative](/wiki/Global_Reporting_Initiative "Global Reporting Initiative"), but these reports are voluntary and unverified. Miscellaneous other certification programs exist for various projects, typically through nonprofit groups.[[75]](#cite_note-RocksHardPlaces-75): 185–86

The purpose of a 2012 EPS PEAKS paper[[102]](#cite_note-102) was to provide evidence on policies managing [ecological costs](/wiki/Environmental_economics "Environmental economics") and maximize [socio-economic](/wiki/Socioeconomics "Socioeconomics") benefits of mining using host country regulatory initiatives. It found existing literature suggesting that donors encourage developing countries to:

* Make the environment-poverty link and introduce cutting-edge wealth measures and [natural capital](/wiki/Natural_capital "Natural capital") accounts.
* Reform old taxes in line with more recent financial innovation, engage directly with the companies, enact land use and impact assessments, and incorporate specialized support and standards agencies.
* Set in play transparency and community participation initiatives using the wealth accrued.

### Waste

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mining&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Waste")]

Further information: [Tailings](/wiki/Tailings "Tailings")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c5/Teghut_Mine_-_waste_rock_storage.jpg/250px-Teghut_Mine_-_waste_rock_storage.jpg)](/wiki/File:Teghut_Mine_-_waste_rock_storage.jpg)

Location of waste rock storage (center) at Teghut (village) Copper-Molybdenum Mine in [Armenia](/wiki/Armenia "Armenia")'s northern Lori province

Ore mills generate large amounts of waste, called [tailings](/wiki/Tailings "Tailings").[[103]](#cite_note-103) For example, 99 tons of waste is generated per ton of [copper](/wiki/Copper "Copper"), with even higher ratios in [gold mining](/wiki/Gold_mining "Gold mining") – because only 5.3 g of gold is extracted per ton of ore, a ton of gold produces 200,000 tons of tailings.[[104]](#cite_note-104) (As time goes on and richer deposits are exhausted – and technology improves – this number is going down to .5 g and less.) These tailings can be toxic. Tailings, which are usually produced as a [slurry](/wiki/Slurry "Slurry"), are most commonly dumped into ponds made from naturally existing valleys.[[105]](#cite_note-EPADesignDams-105) These ponds are secured by impoundments ([dams](/wiki/Dam "Dam") or [embankment dams](/wiki/Embankment_dam "Embankment dam")).[[105]](#cite_note-EPADesignDams-105) In 2000, it was estimated that 3,500 tailings impoundments existed, and that every year, 2 to 5 major [failures](/wiki/Tailings_dam_failure "Tailings dam failure") and 35 minor failures occurred.[[106]](#cite_note-StewardDams-106) For example, in the [Marcopper mining disaster](/wiki/Marcopper_mining_disaster "Marcopper mining disaster") at least 2 million tons of tailings were released into a local river.[[106]](#cite_note-StewardDams-106) In 2015, [Barrick Gold](/wiki/Barrick_Gold "Barrick Gold") Corporation spilled over 1 million liters of [cyanide](/wiki/Cyanide "Cyanide") into a total of five rivers in Argentina near their [Veladero mine](/wiki/Veladero_mine "Veladero mine").[[107]](#cite_note-107) Since 2007 in central Finland, the Talvivaara Terrafame polymetal mine's waste effluent and leaks of saline mine water have resulted in ecological collapse of a nearby lake.[[108]](#cite_note-108) Subaqueous tailings disposal is another option.[[105]](#cite_note-EPADesignDams-105) The mining industry has argued that submarine tailings disposal (STD), which disposes of tailings in the sea, is ideal because it avoids the risks of tailings ponds. The practice is illegal in the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States") and [Canada](/wiki/Canada "Canada"), but it is used in the developing world.[[109]](#cite_note-109)

The waste is classified as either sterile or mineralized, with acid-generating potential, and the movement and storage of this material form a major part of the mine planning process. When an economic cut-off determines the mineralised package, the near-grade mineralised waste is usually dumped separately, with a view to later treatment should market conditions change, and it becomes economically viable. [Civil engineering](/wiki/Civil_engineering "Civil engineering") design parameters are used for waste dump design, with special conditions applying to high-rainfall and seismically active areas. Waste dump designs must meet all regulatory requirements of the country in whose jurisdiction the mine is located. It is also common practice to rehabilitate dumps to an internationally acceptable standard, which, in some cases, means applying higher standards than the local regulatory standard.[[106]](#cite_note-StewardDams-106)

## Human Rights and Environmental Justice

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mining&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Human Rights and Environmental Justice")]

In addition to the [environmental impacts](/wiki/Environmental_impacts "Environmental impacts") of mining processes, a prominent criticism of this form of extractive practice and of mining companies concerns the [human rights](/wiki/Human_rights "Human rights") abuses occurring at mining sites and in [communities](/wiki/Communities "Communities") near them.[[110]](#cite_note-110) Frequently, despite being protected by International [Labor rights](/wiki/Labor_rights "Labor rights"), miners are not given appropriate equipment to provide them with protection from possible mine collapse or from harmful [pollutants](/wiki/Pollutants "Pollutants") and [chemicals](/wiki/Chemicals "Chemicals") expelled during the mining process, work in inhumane conditions spending numerous hours working in extreme heat, darkness and 14 hour workdays with no allocated time for breaks.[[111]](#cite_note-111) In addition, many of these legal structures disproportionally harm indigenous communities.

### Regulation and governance

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mining&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Regulation and governance")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/08/EITI_Global_Conference_2016.jpg/250px-EITI_Global_Conference_2016.jpg)](/wiki/File:EITI_Global_Conference_2016.jpg)

[EITI](/wiki/EITI "EITI") Global Conference 2016

New regulations and a process of legislative reforms aim to improve the harmonization and stability of the mining sector in mineral-rich countries.[[112]](#cite_note-112) New legislation for mining industry in African countries still appears to be an issue, but has the potential to be solved, when a consensus is reached on the best approach.[[113]](#cite_note-Lucia-113) By the beginning of the 21st century, the booming and increasingly complex mining sector in mineral-rich countries was providing only slight benefits to local communities, especially given the sustainability issues. Increasing debate and influence by [NGOs](/wiki/NGOs "NGOs") and local communities called for new approaches which would also include disadvantaged communities, and work towards [sustainable development](/wiki/Sustainable_development "Sustainable development") even after [mine closure](/wiki/Mine_closure "Mine closure") (including transparency and revenue management). By the early 2000s, community development issues and resettlement had become mainstream concerns in World Bank mining projects.[[113]](#cite_note-Lucia-113) Mining-industry expansion after mineral prices increased in 2003, and also potential fiscal revenues in those countries created an omission in the other economic sectors in terms of finances and development. Furthermore, this highlighted the regional and local demand for mining revenues and the inability of sub-national governments to use them effectively. The [Fraser Institute](/wiki/Fraser_Institute "Fraser Institute") (a Canadian think tank) has highlighted[*[clarification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*] the environmental protection laws in developing countries, as well as voluntary efforts by mining companies to improve their environmental impact.[[114]](#cite_note-114)

In 2007, the [Extractive Industries Transparency Initiative](/wiki/Extractive_Industries_Transparency_Initiative "Extractive Industries Transparency Initiative") (EITI) was mainstreamed[*[clarification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*] in all countries cooperating with the World Bank in mining industry reform.[[113]](#cite_note-Lucia-113) The EITI operates and was implemented with the support of the EITI multi-donor trust fund, managed by the World Bank.[[115]](#cite_note-115) The EITI aims to increase transparency in transactions between governments and companies in extractive industries[[116]](#cite_note-116) by monitoring the revenues and benefits between industries and recipient governments. The entrance process is voluntary for each country and is monitored by multiple stakeholders, including governments, private companies, and civil society representatives, responsible for disclosure and dissemination of the reconciliation report;[[113]](#cite_note-Lucia-113) however, the competitive disadvantage of company-by-company public report is, for some of the businesses in Ghana at least, the main constraint.[[117]](#cite_note-117) Therefore, the outcome assessment in terms of failure or success of the new EITI regulation does not only "rest on the government's shoulders" but also on civil society and companies.[[118]](#cite_note-Owona,_S.O.-118)

However, implementation has issues; inclusion or exclusion of [artisanal mining](/wiki/Artisanal_mining "Artisanal mining") and small-scale mining (ASM) from the EITI, and how to deal with "non-cash" payments made by companies to subnational governments. Furthermore, the disproportionate revenues the mining industry can bring to the comparatively small number of people that it employs,[[119]](#cite_note-Lulu-119) causes other problems, like a lack of investment in other less lucrative sectors, leading to swings in government revenue because of volatility in the oil markets. Artisanal mining is clearly an issue in EITI Countries such as the Central African Republic, D.R. Congo, Guinea, Liberia, and Sierra Leone – i.e., almost half of the mining countries implementing the EITI.[[119]](#cite_note-Lulu-119) Among other things, the limited scope of the EITI involving disparity in terms of knowledge of the industry and negotiation skills, thus far, flexibility of the policy (e.g., liberty of the countries to expand beyond the minimum requirements and adapt it to their needs), creates another risk of unsuccessful implementation. Increased public awareness, with the government acting as a bridge between the public and the initiative to ensure a successful policy outcome, is an important element to consider.[[120]](#cite_note-120)

### Child labor

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mining&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Child labor")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/ba/Breaker_Boys_1.jpg/250px-Breaker_Boys_1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Breaker_Boys_1.jpg)

[Breaker boys](/wiki/Breaker_boys "Breaker boys"): child workers who broke down coal at a mine in South [Pittston](/wiki/Pittston "Pittston"), [Pennsylvania](/wiki/Pennsylvania "Pennsylvania"), United States in the early 20th century

Included within the human rights abuses that occur during mining processes are instances of [child labor](/wiki/Child_labour "Child labour"). These instances have sparked widespread criticism of mining [cobalt](/wiki/Cobalt "Cobalt"), a mineral essential for powering modern technologies such as [laptops](/wiki/Laptops "Laptops"), [smartphones](/wiki/Smartphones "Smartphones"), and [electric vehicles](/wiki/Electric_vehicles "Electric vehicles"). Many of these cases of child laborers are found in locations such as the [Democratic Republic of Congo](/wiki/Democratic_Republic_of_Congo "Democratic Republic of Congo"), South Africa, the Philippines, Australia, and Brazil. Reports have risen of children carrying sacks of cobalt weighing 25 kg from small mines to local traders[[121]](#cite_note-121) being paid for their work only in food and accommodation. Many companies, such as [Apple](/wiki/Apple_Inc. "Apple Inc."), [Google](/wiki/Google "Google"), [Microsoft](/wiki/Microsoft "Microsoft"), and [Tesla](/wiki/Tesla,_Inc. "Tesla, Inc.") have been implicated in lawsuits brought by families whose children were severely injured or killed during mining activities in Congo.[[122]](#cite_note-122) In December 2019, 14 Congolese families filed a lawsuit against [Glencore](/wiki/Glencore "Glencore"), a mining company which supplies the essential cobalt to these multinational corporations with allegations of negligence that led to the deaths of children or injuries such as broken spines, emotional distress and forced labor.[[123]](#cite_note-123)

### Indigenous peoples

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mining&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Indigenous peoples")]

There have also been instances of killings and evictions attributed to conflicts with mining companies. Almost a third of 227 murders in 2020 were of Indigenous peoples rights activists on the frontlines of [climate change activism](/wiki/Individual_action_on_climate_change "Individual action on climate change") linked to [logging](/wiki/Logging "Logging"), mining, large-scale [agribusiness](/wiki/Agribusiness "Agribusiness"), hydroelectric dams, and other infrastructure, according to [Global Witness](/wiki/Global_Witness "Global Witness").[[124]](#cite_note-124)

Struggles over access to land define the relationship between indigenous peoples and mining.

In the Democratic Republic of the Congo, there are issues with both evictions and a lack of compensation. There are "forced evictions taking place as companies that seek to expand industrial-scale copper and cobalt mining projects are wrecking lives" and "following protests, in 2019 Chemaf, the company responsible for the industrial mining projects, agreed to pay via the local authority US$1.5 million, but some former residents received as little as US$300."[[125]](#cite_note-:3-125) According to the US government report, "Corruption and official complicity in trafficking crimes remained significant concerns, impeding law enforcement and judicial action. Sources reported widespread complicity, including allegations that government officials directly engaged in trafficking, helped facilitate the crime, and obstructed justice. Government officials accepted bribes to overlook labor abuses, including forced labor and the worst forms of child labor, in the mining sector. Perpetrators of sexual violence, including sex trafficking, were rarely held accountable, and security forces continued to sexually abuse and exploit victims, including children, with impunity."[[125]](#cite_note-:3-125) There is little information about what citizens of DRC are doing in retaliation.

In South Africa, according to health scientist Brad Kistanasamy and their peers, their "mineral resources have produced, and continue to produce, enormous economic wealth; yet decades of colonialism, apartheid, capital flight, and challenges in the neoliberal post-apartheid era have resulted in high rates of occupational lung disease and low rates of compensation for ex-miners and their families."[[126]](#cite_note-126) According to Amnesty International, "By the end of 2017, 111,166 miners had received compensation (of which 55,864 were for permanent lung impairment, and another 52,473 for tuberculosis), however 107,714 compensable claims remained unpaid." To address current lead poisoning, there is a current class action "by residents of Kabwe (Zambia) against the mining company Anglo American in South Africa. The claimants allege severe lead poisoning caused by mining operations in Kabwe. SALC and Amnesty International submit that the case represents a pivotal moment for corporate accountability and the rights of communities affected by transnational and transgenerational harm."[[127]](#cite_note-127)

In Australia, the Aboriginal [Bininj](/wiki/Bininj "Bininj") said mining posed a threat to their living culture and could damage sacred heritage sites.[[128]](#cite_note-128)[[129]](#cite_note-129) Although Australia is one of the most profitable mining countries, "Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islanders (henceforward referred to as Indigenous Australians) have not shared in this bounty equitably, with social and economic disadvantage exacerbated where mining activity is the greatest.[[130]](#cite_note-130) As of now, "Australian restoration networks are still poorly articulated and not in the position to stimulate an inclusive sector aligned with democratic and participatory engagements with Indigenous Australians."[[131]](#cite_note-131)

In the Philippines, an anti-mining movement has raised concerns regarding "the total disregard for [Indigenous communities'] ancestral land rights."[[132]](#cite_note-Tartlet-2001-132) According to Geographic Scientist William Holden and their peers, "Those unfamiliar with indigenous culture may mistakenly believe that mining poses minimal risks, since indigenous peoples have little income or wealth to lose and [suffer from] high unemployment,"[[133]](#cite_note-:4-133) when in reality "such a view is incorrect because 'the wealth that supports the sustainability of their culture is found in institutions, environmental knowledge, local resources, and especially in land embellished with cultural meaning."[[133]](#cite_note-:4-133) [Ifugao peoples](/wiki/Igorot_people "Igorot people")' opposition to mining led a governor to proclaim a ban on mining operations in Mountain Province, Philippines.[[132]](#cite_note-Tartlet-2001-132)

In Brazil, more than 170 tribes organized a march to oppose controversial attempts to strip back indigenous land rights and open their territories to mining operations.[[134]](#cite_note-134) The [United Nations Commission on Human Rights](/wiki/United_Nations_Commission_on_Human_Rights "United Nations Commission on Human Rights") has called on Brazil's Supreme Court to uphold Indigenous land rights to prevent exploitation by mining groups and industrial agriculture.[[135]](#cite_note-135) For issues such as gold mining and illegal mining, " uniform law enforcement is required to prevent the waterbed effect (when fighting miners somewhere, they pop up elsewhere); the unhinged miners have not been hinged yet. In addition, gold trading laws need to be strengthened to ensure accountability for the illegal gold trade in the Amazon.[[136]](#cite_note-136)

### Lack of Safety Enforcement

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mining&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Lack of Safety Enforcement")]

There is a lack of law enforcement in various countries. For example,  "although South Africa has strong regulations to avoid potential mining impacts, lack of enforcement and corruption have been barriers to combating social and environmental injustices,"[[137]](#cite_note-137) leading to unsafe working conditions for many miners (see Safety Section). In the DRC, "more than half of the DRC's natural resource exports are not officially recorded because of tax evasion and state institutions' lack of governance capacity," and "90% of minerals come from artisanal miners,"[[138]](#cite_note-138) which are indigenous people. In Ghana, similar safety issues exist. This is because "inadequate monitoring of the operations and lack of regulatory enforcement by the Minerals Commission of Ghana are major contributing factors to the environmental, safety, and national security issues of the operations,"[[139]](#cite_note-139) implying a failure of enforcement from local authorities, similar to these other countries.

### Corporate Negligence and Lack of Regulation Towards Indigenous Peoples

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mining&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Corporate Negligence and Lack of Regulation Towards Indigenous Peoples")]

There have already been international loopholes that threaten the safety of indigenous miners (see safety regulations). These often exist due to "multiple factors such as colonial legacies, economic inequalities, global power imbalances, inadequate governance, regulatory weaknesses, and insufficient community participation."[[140]](#cite_note-:5-140) In these countries, mining legislation and regulations either don't include the scope of compensation for indigenous populations, or aren't enforced. For example, the Mines and Minerals Act in Zimbabwe "does not provide for direct compensation to inhabitants of Communal Lands, making them particularly vulnerable."[[141]](#cite_note-141) Although there are laws against child labor in the DRC,[[142]](#cite_note-142) "In the DRC cobalt belt, field research describes 'subterranean slavery' in which children descend 10–30m pits to fill sacks of ore for as little as US $1–2 per day. Violence has intensified rather than fallen since U.S. conflict-mineral legislation, with statistically significant spikes in armed incidents around mine sites,"[[140]](#cite_note-:5-140) which is most likely not enforced due to financial benefit. For justice to be secured, laws need to both encapsulate justice for indigenous communities and enforce them, despite any financial incentives to do otherwise.[[143]](#cite_note-143)

## Mining and Land Rights

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mining&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: Mining and Land Rights")]

Mining operations often intersect with land rights in complex and sometimes contentious ways. In many countries, land ownership and mineral resource ownership are legally separated, with mineral rights usually vested in the state, while individuals or communities may own land.[[144]](#cite_note-144)

This creates a situation in which communities may have legal ownership of the surface land but limited control over mineral extraction beneath it.

Customary land tenure, prevalent in many rural areas, often governs land use and inheritance.[[145]](#cite_note-:0-145)

Mining in these areas can disrupt farming, grazing, and culturally significant sites, and communities without formal titles may be vulnerable to displacement or inadequate compensation.[[145]](#cite_note-:0-145)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/69/Chuquicamata-002.jpg/250px-Chuquicamata-002.jpg)](/wiki/File:Chuquicamata-002.jpg)

[Chuquicamata](/wiki/Chuquicamata "Chuquicamata"), [Chile](/wiki/Chile "Chile"), site of the largest circumference and second deepest [open pit](/wiki/Open-pit_mining "Open-pit mining") [copper](/wiki/Copper "Copper") mine in the world

As of 2019, [Mponeng](/wiki/Mponeng_Gold_Mine "Mponeng Gold Mine") is the world's second deepest mine from ground level, reaching a depth of 4 km (2.5 mi) below ground level. The trip from the surface to the bottom of the mine takes over an hour. It is a [gold mine](/wiki/Gold_mine "Gold mine") in [South Africa](/wiki/South_Africa "South Africa")'s [Gauteng](/wiki/Gauteng "Gauteng") province. Previously known as [Western Deep Levels](/wiki/Western_Deep_Levels "Western Deep Levels") #1 Shaft, the underground and surface works were commissioned in 1987. The mine is considered one of the largest gold mines in the world.

The Moab Khutsong gold mine in [North West Province (South Africa)](/wiki/North_West_Province_(South_Africa) "North West Province (South Africa)") has the world's longest winding steel wire rope, which can lower workers to 3,054 metres (10,020 ft) in a single uninterrupted four-minute journey.[[146]](#cite_note-Mining_and_minerals_in_South_Africa-146)

The deepest mine in Europe is the 16th shaft of the uranium mines in [Příbram](/wiki/P%C5%99%C3%ADbram "Příbram"), [Czech Republic](/wiki/Czech_Republic "Czech Republic"), at 1,838 metres (6,030 ft).[[147]](#cite_note-DeepestEurope-147) Second is [Bergwerk Saar](/w/index.php?title=Bergwerk_Saar&action=edit&redlink=1 "Bergwerk Saar (page does not exist)") in [Saarland](/wiki/Saarland "Saarland"), [Germany](/wiki/Germany "Germany"), at 1,750 metres (5,740 ft). [[148]](#cite_note-148)

The deepest open-pit mine in the world is [Bingham Canyon Mine](/wiki/Bingham_Canyon_Mine "Bingham Canyon Mine") in [Bingham Canyon](/wiki/Bingham_Canyon "Bingham Canyon"), [Utah](/wiki/Utah "Utah"), United States, at over 1,200 metres (3,900 ft). The largest and second-deepest open-pit copper mine in the world is [Chuquicamata](/wiki/Chuquicamata "Chuquicamata") in northern [Chile](/wiki/Chile "Chile"), at 900 metres (3,000 ft), which annually produces 443,000 tons of copper and 20,000 tons of molybdenum.[[149]](#cite_note-149)[[150]](#cite_note-150)[[151]](#cite_note-151)

The deepest open-pit mine with respect to [sea level](/wiki/Sea_level "Sea level") is [Tagebau Hambach](/wiki/Tagebau_Hambach "Tagebau Hambach") in Germany, where the base of the pit is 299 metres (981 ft) below sea level.[[152]](#cite_note-152)

The largest underground mine is [Kiirunavaara Mine](/wiki/Kiruna_Mine "Kiruna Mine") in [Kiruna](/wiki/Kiruna "Kiruna"), [Sweden](/wiki/Sweden "Sweden"). With 450 kilometres (280 miles) of roads, 40 million tonnes of annually produced ore, and a depth of 1,270 metres (4,170 ft), it is also one of the most modern underground mines. The deepest borehole in the world is [Kola Superdeep Borehole](/wiki/Kola_Superdeep_Borehole "Kola Superdeep Borehole") at 12,262 metres (40,230 ft), but this is connected to [scientific drilling](/wiki/Scientific_drilling "Scientific drilling"), not mining.[[153]](#cite_note-153)

## Metal reserves and recycling

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mining&action=edit&section=34 "Edit section: Metal reserves and recycling")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f0/NatCopper.jpg/250px-NatCopper.jpg)](/wiki/File:NatCopper.jpg)

Macro of [native copper](/wiki/Native_copper "Native copper") about 1+1⁄2 inches (4 cm) in size

Main articles: [Landfill mining](/wiki/Landfill_mining "Landfill mining") and [Recycling](/wiki/Recycling "Recycling")

See also: [Conflict resource](/wiki/Conflict_resource "Conflict resource") and [List of critical mineral raw materials](/wiki/List_of_critical_mineral_raw_materials "List of critical mineral raw materials")

During the 20th century, the variety of [metals](/wiki/Metals "Metals") used in society increased. Today, the development of major nations such as China and India, along with technological advances, fuels an ever-greater demand. The result is that metal mining activities are expanding, and more and more of the world's metal stocks are above ground in use rather than below ground as unused reserves. An example is the in-use stock of [copper](/wiki/Copper "Copper"). Between 1932 and 1999, copper in use in the US rose from 73 kilograms (161 lb) to 238 kilograms (525 lb) per person.[[154]](#cite_note-unep-154)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d1/Abandoned_Metal_Recycling_Plant.png/250px-Abandoned_Metal_Recycling_Plant.png)](/wiki/File:Abandoned_Metal_Recycling_Plant.png)

A former metal recycling plant in [South Carolina](/wiki/South_Carolina "South Carolina").

95% of the energy used to make aluminium from [bauxite](/wiki/Bauxite "Bauxite") ore is saved by using recycled material.[[155]](#cite_note-155) However, levels of metal recycling are generally low. In 2010, the [International Resource Panel](/wiki/International_Resource_Panel "International Resource Panel"), hosted by the [United Nations Environment Programme](/wiki/United_Nations_Environment_Programme "United Nations Environment Programme") (UNEP), published reports on metal stocks that exist within society[[156]](#cite_note-metal_stocks-156) and their recycling rates.[[154]](#cite_note-unep-154)

The report's authors observed that the metal stocks in society can serve as substantial anthropogenic mines above ground.[[156]](#cite_note-metal_stocks-156) However, they warned that the recycling rates of some rare metals used in applications such as mobile phones, battery packs for hybrid cars, and fuel cells are so low that unless future end-of-life recycling rates are dramatically stepped up these critical metals will become unavailable for use in modern technology.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

As recycling rates are low and so much metal has already been extracted, some [landfill](/wiki/Landfill "Landfill") now contain higher concentrations of metal than the mines themselves.[[157]](#cite_note-157) This is especially true of [aluminum](/wiki/Aluminium "Aluminium"), used in cans, and [precious metals](/wiki/Precious_metal "Precious metal"), found in discarded electronics.[[158]](#cite_note-158) Furthermore, waste after 15 years has still not broken down, so less processing would be required when compared to mining ores. A study undertaken by [Cranfield University](/wiki/Cranfield_University "Cranfield University") has found £360 million of metals could be mined from just four [landfill sites.](/wiki/Landfill "Landfill")[[159]](#cite_note-159) There is also up to 20 MJ/kg of energy in waste, potentially making the re-extraction more profitable.[[160]](#cite_note-160) However, although the first landfill mine opened in [Tel Aviv](/wiki/Tel_Aviv "Tel Aviv"), Israel in 1953, little work has followed due to the abundance of accessible ores.[[161]](#cite_note-161)
